nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia (>=3 nights/week for >=3 months) and functional impairment.
Elicits core sleep history: onset/maintenance/early awakening, duration, frequency, daytime consequences, sleep schedule, naps, and sleep environment.

Screens for common contributors/comorbidities before treatment: depression/anxiety, substance use (alcohol/cannabis), caffeine/nicotine, pain, thyroid symptoms, medication-induced insomnia (stimulants, SSRIs/SNRIs, steroids, decongestants), and shift work.

Screens for other sleep disorders that change management: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome/PLMD (urge to move legs, evening worsening), circadian rhythm disorder.
Prior to prescribing, performs required safety checks: (1) allergy check, (2) drug interaction check, (3) contraindication check; does not prescribe if a critical safety issue is identified.
Explains treatment plan and explicitly includes the term "benzodiazepine" while emphasizing it is short-term/lowest dose and not first-line long-term therapy.

Provides nonpharmacologic counseling consistent with CBT-I principles: stimulus control, sleep restriction concepts, consistent wake time, limiting naps, reducing caffeine/alcohol, and sleep hygiene; offers/arranges CBT-I.

Counsels on benzodiazepine adverse effects and safety: next-day sedation, falls, cognitive impairment, anterograde amnesia, complex sleep behaviors, respiratory depression risk when combined with CNS depressants, and dependence/tapering if prolonged use.

Avoids benzodiazepines or uses extreme caution in high-risk contexts (e.g., pregnancy, older adults, history of substance use disorder, significant pulmonary disease/OSA risk, concomitant opioids) and documents rationale if used.

Creates a clear follow-up expectation (even if not a required tool): reassess within 1-2 weeks for benefit/side effects and plan discontinuation; advises return precautions for severe adverse effects or worsening mood/suicidality.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose combined factor V and VIII deficiency, initiate FFP + tranexamic acid with required safety checks, and refer to hematology/HTC.
must_collect: history of prior bleeding with dental work/surgery
medication list (anticoagulants, antiplatelets, NSAIDs, hormonal therapy)
allergy history (medications and prior transfusion reactions)
thromboembolism history and renal disease (for tranexamic acid contraindications/dosing)
pregnancy status or upcoming procedure (if relevant)
steps: Explain that the condition is a bleeding disorder due to low factor V and factor VIII.
Record diagnosis: combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII.
Before prescribing, perform allergy and contraindication checks; consider drug interactions.
Prescribe fresh frozen plasma for factor V replacement for active bleeding/peri-procedural management and outline transfusion monitoring.
Prescribe tranexamic acid as adjunct for mucosal bleeding with renal adjustment and contraindication screening.
Refer to hematology/hemophilia treatment center for confirmatory workup and comprehensive management plan.

acceptable_variants.description: Escalation-first variant for severe/ongoing bleeding: treat as urgent while still completing outcomes.
steps: State urgency and advise ED/urgent evaluation for severe bleeding while continuing plan.
Record diagnosis: combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII.
Perform allergy and contraindication checks prior to therapies.
Prescribe FFP and tranexamic acid with monitoring and precautions.
Refer urgently to hematology/HTC for immediate guidance and factor replacement strategy.

acceptable_variants.description: Peri-procedural planning variant: patient not actively bleeding but has upcoming surgery/dental work.
steps: Explain diagnosis and bleeding risk with procedures.
Record diagnosis: combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII.
Perform allergy and contraindication checks.
Prescribe tranexamic acid for peri-procedural mucosal bleeding prophylaxis when appropriate and plan FFP peri-procedurally per protocol.
Refer to hematology/HTC for peri-operative hemostasis plan (including FVIII concentrate considerations).

acceptable_variants.description: Contraindication-adjusted variant: tranexamic acid not appropriate.
steps: Explain diagnosis and that it is a bleeding disorder.
Record diagnosis: combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII.
Identify tranexamic acid contraindication (e.g., gross hematuria/upper urinary tract bleeding or high thrombotic risk) during contraindication check.
Prescribe FFP with monitoring; omit or adjust tranexamic acid and document reasoning.
Refer to hematology/HTC for alternative adjuncts and definitive management.
